experiments.hypothesis: Optimizing MA window parameters (fast=8, slow=25) and reducing short exposure will improve signal timing
experiments.approach: Grid search over fast=[5,8,12] and slow=[20,25,30] combinations, test short ratios [-0.5, -0.3, 0]
experiments.expected_outcome: Sharpe Ratio improvement from 0.84 to >1.0 through better entry/exit timing
steps: Step 1: Create optimized_ma function with configurable fast/slow/short params
Step 3: Run python run_backtest.py and capture metric
experiments.execution_log: Tested 9 parameter combinations. fast=8, slow=25 showed best improvement. fast=5 was too noisy, slow=30 too laggy. Reducing short exposure from -0.5 to -0.3 was a key improvement — full short was too aggressive.
experiments.learnings: Two independent improvements found: (1) Shorter fast window (8 vs 10) catches trends earlier. slow=25 provides better noise filtering than 20. (2) Reducing short ratio from -0.5 to -0.3 is a 'free' improvement — baseline was too aggressive on shorts. Parameter sensitivity is moderate — results robust within fast=[7-9], slow=[23-27].

experiments.hypothesis: Adding MACD signal as confirmation will reduce false crossover signals
experiments.approach: Implement MACD(12,26,9) and require both MA crossover AND MACD histogram positive for buy signals
experiments.expected_outcome: Reduce false signals by 30%, improve Sharpe from 1.37 to >1.5
steps: Step 1: Implement MACD calculation in strategies.py
Step 2: Add MACD confirmation to signal generation
experiments.execution_log: MACD confirmation was too restrictive — missed 40% of profitable entries. The lag from MACD(12,26,9) combined with MA crossover created double-lag, entering positions too late.
experiments.learnings: DEAD END: MACD + MA crossover creates double-lag problem. Both are trend-following indicators with similar lag characteristics. Need a DIFFERENT type of signal (momentum/rate-of-change) for confirmation, not another lagging trend indicator.

experiments.hypothesis: RSI-based position sizing will reduce exposure during extreme conditions and improve risk-adjusted returns
experiments.approach: Scale position size based on RSI(14) distance from 50 — full size at RSI=50, reduced at extremes
experiments.expected_outcome: Better risk management, improve Sharpe from 1.37 to >1.5
steps: Step 2: Create position sizing function based on RSI distance from 50
Step 3: Run backtest and compare metrics
experiments.execution_log: RSI-based sizing produced marginal degradation (1.37 → 1.33). The position size reduction at RSI extremes removed exposure during some of the strongest trending moves, which were profitable.
experiments.learnings: In momentum-driven data, RSI extremes often coincide with strong trends that should be HELD, not reduced. RSI as a filter/sizer works AGAINST trend-following strategies. Key insight: don't fight the trend with mean-reversion indicators. Use momentum-type indicators that CONFIRM the trend instead.

experiments.hypothesis: Adding a 2% stop-loss will protect against large drawdowns
experiments.approach: Implement per-trade stop-loss at -2% from entry price
experiments.expected_outcome: Reduce max drawdown by 30%, maintain or improve Sharpe
steps: Step 1: Track entry prices for each position
Step 2: Implement stop-loss check against intraday low
experiments.execution_log: Implementation caused index alignment errors when stop-loss triggered mid-bar. The simple vectorized backtest framework doesn't support intra-bar exits. Would need event-driven backtester (e.g., backtrader) for proper stop-loss simulation.
experiments.learnings: FRAMEWORK LIMITATION: Current vectorized backtest cannot simulate intra-bar exits (stop-loss, take-profit). These features require an event-driven architecture. DO NOT attempt stop-loss/take-profit without migrating to backtrader or similar. This is a framework limitation, not a strategy problem.

experiments.hypothesis: Momentum confirmation with asymmetric position sizing will improve signal quality by aligning entry size with trend conviction
experiments.approach: Use 10-day price momentum to classify signals into strong/moderate/cautious. Full position on confirmed uptrend + positive momentum, reduced position on conflicting signals, flat on counter-trend momentum.
experiments.expected_outcome: Higher conviction entries, Sharpe from 1.37 to >1.5
steps: Step 2: Create ma_momentum strategy with three conviction tiers
Step 3: Implement asymmetric short logic (short only on confirmed downtrend, flat on conflicting)
experiments.execution_log: Momentum confirmation significantly improved signal quality. Three key improvements: (1) Strong buy signals (trend + momentum aligned) captured best moves at full size. (2) Cautious positions during momentum divergence avoided whipsaws. (3) Going flat instead of short during bear+positive-momentum periods eliminated the most costly false shorts.
experiments.learnings: Momentum (rate of change) is the RIGHT confirmation for trend-following — unlike RSI, it CONFIRMS the trend rather than fighting it. Asymmetric sizing is more powerful than binary signals. The biggest single win was NOT shorting during conflicting signals (bear trend + positive momentum = flat). Position sizing by conviction level is a general principle worth applying to future strategies.

experiments.hypothesis: Adaptive MA windows based on volatility regime will improve performance in varying market conditions
experiments.approach: Use ATR(20) to detect volatility regime, use shorter windows in low-vol (fast=6,slow=15) and longer in high-vol (fast=12,slow=30)
experiments.expected_outcome: Better regime adaptation, push Sharpe beyond 1.89
steps: Step 1: Calculate ATR(20) and define volatility regimes
experiments.execution_log: Regime-switching caused excessive signal changes at volatility boundaries. The strategy oscillated between parameter sets when ATR hovered near the threshold, generating false signals. Sharpe dropped to 1.15.
experiments.learnings: PARTIAL DEAD END: Simple threshold-based regime switching creates boundary instability. The oscillation at the threshold is the core problem. If retrying: (a) use hysteresis (different thresholds for entering vs exiting regime), (b) smooth interpolation between parameter sets instead of hard switching, (c) longer ATR lookback (40+). Note: target (1.5) is already exceeded at 1.89, so further optimization has diminishing returns.
